name: Avenc de sa Finestreta
starting_point: Puerto de Pollensa, UTM coordinates DATUM ETRS89 508230 4418185
clothing: Coveralls, gloves, and knee pads essential
equipment: Ropes: 1 x 40 m, 1 x 65 m. Slings: 7. Carabiners: 12. Bolts: 5. Some rope protectors.
description: Discovered in 2015 at the base of a cliff near dwarf palms, this 73 m deep sinkhole required extensive widening at various points. It features narrow passages, multiple vertical shafts, and a series of chambers descending to the CO2 Room at maximum depth. A challenging cave requiring both vertical progression skills and the ability to navigate very tight passages, where any rescue would be extremely difficult.
full_description:
Description

Located at the base of a cliff and hidden next to a group of Chamaerops humilis (palmetto or dwarf palm), it was discovered by Pedro Martín in 2015. After arduous clearing and widening work carried out at different points of the cavity by the discoverer in the company of Antonio Tarazaga, that same year the first topographic survey was conducted under the direction of Rafael Minguillón, covering the first 22 m of depth of the sinkhole. Subsequent clearing and widening have allowed it to reach its current depth of 73 m.

As soon as one enters through the small access mouth, one must face the first narrow passage, which once overcome leaves us above a small shaft of approximately two meters in depth. A small window leads us into a short crawlway that will soon allow us to reach the head of the shaft that will place us seven meters lower, where we will set up another anchor point to reach the Gatera Room, at -22 m.

Four meters before reaching the deepest zone of the shaft, a level opens to the left and right. To the east, one can descend to a small obstructed shaft. From this location, one can observe how the exterior light is visible in the overhead zone. This is the inaccessible entrance to the sinkhole, but it can be used to pass some of the equipment we will need into the interior of the cavity. To the west of this level and after a small climb, one can access a beautiful room featuring beautiful curtains, some of them of large dimensions.

In the SE zone of the room, a crawlway of approximately ten meters, the third difficulty in the cavity, absorbs the water that reaches this point of the sinkhole. In the overhead zone of the crawlway, the erosive channel that once allowed the continuity of the water as well as the development of the sinkhole is clearly visible. At the end of the crawlway, a new shaft of 18.5 m in depth opens, placing us at the -41 m level, on a platform of 3 x 4 m — this is the Sala des Fang (Mud Room). To the east of the room, a small sinkhole opens that, after overcoming the Pas des Patiment (Passage of Suffering), will allow us to look out through a small window onto the Pou des Nius d'Oroneta (Swallow Nests Well); two spits located on a column will allow us to install the rope to descend along a stone cascade to the Sala des Paleonivel (Paleolevel Room), which is located 18 m below, and from there to the SE a new shaft exceeding twelve meters will place us in the deepest zone of the cavity, the Sala des CO2 (CO2 Room), where mud abounds in addition to a certain amount of carbon dioxide, and where although the cavity appears to continue through a sinkhole on the west side of the room, the passage becomes impassable.

The Avenc de sa Finestreta is an interesting cavity, since in addition to qualities that allow the practice of recreational caving, both with the use of vertical progression equipment and by testing our ability to overcome very narrow passages — notably the Pas des Patiment, undoubtedly the most difficult, along with the one that gives the sinkhole its name and which is located just one meter from the entrance, whose greatest difficulty comes when exiting the sinkhole. It should be noted that an accident in this cavity would make rescue an extremely laborious and difficult undertaking.
